content.text: What It Is

Programme Increment (PI) Planning is a time-boxed event—typically one to two days—in which all teams contributing to an agentic AI programme plan their work for the next twelve weeks together, surface cross-team dependencies, and commit to a shared set of PI Objectives that represent the programme's expected value delivery for the increment. Adapted from SAFe, PI Planning for agentic AI portfolios is the primary mechanism through which strategic priorities are translated into team-level commitments and through which cross-team dependencies are identified and resolved before they become delivery blockers.

PI Planning is distinct from sprint planning—it operates at a higher level of granularity and a longer time horizon. Where sprint planning commits teams to specific stories for a two-week sprint, PI Planning commits teams to capabilities and milestones for a twelve-week increment. This longer horizon is necessary for agentic AI programmes because many dependencies—model training timelines, evaluation harness development, data pipeline construction—have lead times that exceed a single sprint.

The planning event is not a waterfall planning exercise. Teams are not expected to plan every story in the PI at the planning event; they are expected to plan at a level of detail sufficient to identify cross-team dependencies, estimate capacity commitment, and set PI Objectives. The detailed sprint planning happens within each sprint, informed by the higher-level PI plan. This separation preserves team-level planning autonomy while enabling programme-level coordination.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI programmes without a shared planning event operate on implicit assumptions about what other teams are building and when. A team may spend weeks building an agent capability that depends on a model evaluation score that another team has been tasked to improve, only to discover at integration time that the evaluation work was deprioritised in favour of other work. PI Planning makes these implicit dependencies explicit and provides a structured forum for resolving them before the increment begins.

PI Planning also creates a shared understanding of programme context that cannot be achieved through written communications alone. When all teams in a programme hear the same business vision briefing, the same architecture update, and the same priority rationale simultaneously, they develop a shared mental model of what the programme is trying to achieve and why. This shared context improves the quality of individual team decisions throughout the PI because teams can evaluate their sprint-level choices against a shared understanding of programme direction.
Benefits
Dependency identification:
The programme board that results from team breakouts makes cross-team dependencies visible to all teams simultaneously.
Shared context:
All teams hear the same business vision and architecture context, creating shared mental models that improve individual team decision-making.
Realistic commitment:
Teams commit to PI Objectives based on their own capacity assessment rather than externally imposed plans, improving forecast accuracy.
Conflict resolution:
Resource conflicts and capability overlaps that would otherwise be discovered at integration time are surfaced and resolved at the planning event.
Stakeholder alignment:
Business stakeholders who attend the planning event and review PI Objectives develop accurate expectations about what will be delivered in the increment.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Planning event cost:
A two-day event with all teams and key stakeholders has significant direct cost and opportunity cost.
Remote facilitation complexity:
Distributed teams participating in PI Planning remotely face higher coordination complexity than in-person events.
Planning uncertainty for AI work:
Model training timelines and research outcomes are harder to estimate than conventional software work, reducing the reliability of AI-specific PI commitments.
Twelve-week horizon mismatch:
Some agentic AI programme cycles are shorter than twelve weeks; the PI cadence may not match the programme's natural delivery rhythm.
How It Works

The PI Planning event follows a defined agenda over one to two days. Day one begins with a business context presentation from business owners—covering strategic direction, current metrics, and what the programme needs to accomplish in the upcoming PI—followed by an architecture vision briefing from the system architect covering platform changes, model serving upgrades, and technical constraints for the PI. Product Management then presents the programme backlog and prioritised features for the PI.

Teams then break into separate planning rooms for team breakouts—typically four to six hours—where each team plans their sprint commitments for the full PI, identifies dependencies on other teams, and drafts their PI Objectives. Dependency flags are placed on a shared programme board—a large visual artefact showing all teams and their planned work—connecting work items that have dependencies across teams. The programme board is reviewed by all teams together at the end of day one.

Day two focuses on resolving dependencies and finalising plans. Teams negotiate dependency resolutions—either accepting them as-is, adjusting their plan to accommodate the dependency, or escalating unresolvable conflicts for programme management decision. Risks are identified and ROAM-ed. Each team presents their final PI Objectives, and the ART votes on overall PI confidence. A confidence vote of less than seventy percent triggers additional planning time to address the concerns driving low confidence.

For agentic AI portfolios, PI Planning must accommodate AI-specific work types. Research spikes—time-boxed experiments with a decision as output rather than a feature—should be planned at the team breakout level with an explicit output commitment (a decision or a written evaluation, not a working feature). Model training milestones—which have uncertain timelines—should be planned with explicit buffer and a fallback plan specified in the PI Objective if the milestone is not met.
Architecture

The PI Planning flow begins with the business vision presentation, which establishes the strategic context and business priorities for the increment. The architecture briefing follows, covering platform capabilities, technical constraints, and quality requirements that bound team planning. Teams then break out to plan their increment commitments, draft their PI Objectives, and flag cross-team dependencies on the shared programme board. The dependency review session resolves conflicts and distributes programme board ownership. The PI commitment session closes the event with team PI Objective presentations and an ART-wide confidence vote.
When to Use It
At the start of each twelve-week increment:
The PI Planning event is a recurring ceremony, not a one-time event; it must be held before each PI begins.
When programmes scale beyond three teams:
The coordination value of PI Planning becomes essential when more than three teams have significant dependencies.
When releasing to production on a defined schedule:
Programmes with external release commitments benefit from the shared release planning that PI Planning enables.
In SAFe implementations:
PI Planning is a core SAFe ceremony and is required for organisations implementing SAFe.
How to Implement
Prepare the programme backlog:
Before the PI Planning event, Product Management should have a prepared and prioritised programme backlog covering at least one hundred fifty percent of anticipated PI capacity.
Prepare the architecture vision:
The System Architect should brief the RTE on planned platform updates, technical constraints, and architectural guardrails for the PI.
Secure business owner attendance:
PI Planning is most effective when business owners are present throughout the event to answer questions and prioritise trade-offs; confirm their attendance in advance.
Book the venue or set up virtual facilitation:
For in-person events, a large room where all teams can work simultaneously is required. For virtual events, a virtual collaboration platform with programme board support is necessary.
Facilitate the event:
The RTE facilitates the event; the agenda should be timed and managed actively to prevent overruns in the business context or team breakout phases.
Review and publish PI Objectives:
After the event, publish the final PI Objectives for all teams in a shared location accessible to all programme stakeholders.
Track PI Objectives weekly:
During the PI, track progress against PI Objectives weekly and surface deviations for programme management attention.
Examples

# pi-objectives-template.yaml — PI Objectives for agentic AI teams
teams:
  agent_platform_team:
    pi_objectives:
      - id: "PLT-PI2-001"
        objective: "Deploy model serving v2.0 supporting 500 concurrent inference requests"
        business_value: 9
        committed: true
        milestone_sprint: 4
        success_metric: "Load test passing at 500 rps with p99 < 200ms"
      - id: "PLT-PI2-002"
        objective: "Implement evaluation harness v1.5 with automated regression suite"
        business_value: 8
        committed: true
        milestone_sprint: 6
        success_metric: "100% of v1.4 test cases passing in automated suite"
      - id: "PLT-PI2-003"
        objective: "STRETCH: Deploy caching layer reducing average inference cost by 30%"
        business_value: 7
        committed: false  # stretch objective
        milestone_sprint: 12

  customer_automation_team:
    pi_objectives:
      - id: "CA-PI2-001"
        objective: "Deploy intent routing agent to 100% of chat channel traffic"
        business_value: 10
        committed: true
        milestone_sprint: 8
        dependencies:
          - team: "agent_platform_team"
            item: "PLT-PI2-001"
            needed_by_sprint: 4
        success_metric: "Intent routing accuracy >= 88% on held-out evaluation set"
      - id: "CA-PI2-002"
        objective: "Complete research spike: evaluate retrieval-augmented approach for FAQ agent"
        business_value: 6
        committed: true
        milestone_sprint: 3
        success_metric: "Written decision document: build / buy / defer with evidence"

  risk_analytics_team:
    pi_objectives:
      - id: "RA-PI2-001"
        objective: "Deliver credit risk agent supporting automated decline decisions for tier-3 applicants"
        business_value: 9
        committed: true
        milestone_sprint: 10
        regulatory_gate: "Compliance review required at sprint 8 before proceeding to production"
Real-World Example

An insurance company with six agentic AI teams held their first PI Planning event after months of operating with monthly programme syncs that were consistently overwhelmed by cross-team conflicts. The planning event revealed forty-one cross-team dependencies across the six teams. Of these, thirty-three were resolved during the planning event by adjusting sprint priorities or accepting the dependency with a confirmed delivery date. Eight dependencies were escalated to the Release Train Engineer for programme management resolution.

The RTE resolved four of the eight escalated dependencies by negotiating plan adjustments with the providing teams. Two were parked as known risks with mitigation plans. Two required scope adjustments to the PI Objectives that were approved by Product Management during the event. The first PI run under the new model achieved eighty-one percent of its committed PI Objectives—compared to a baseline of fifty-three percent achieved in the previous quarter under the monthly sync model. The team attributed the improvement primarily to the structured dependency identification and resolution that the PI Planning event enabled.
Common Mistakes
Business owners absent:
PI Planning events where business owners attend only the opening presentation miss the continuous business context that teams need during team breakouts.
Architecture briefing too short:
Teams cannot plan effectively if they do not understand the platform constraints for the PI; shortening the architecture briefing to save time is a false economy.
Insufficient backlog preparation:
Teams that arrive at PI Planning without a prepared, prioritised programme backlog spend the planning event doing backlog refinement instead of planning.
Programme board not owned:
A programme board that is created but not maintained during the PI provides a one-time view of dependencies that becomes stale and misleading.
Confidence vote theatre:
If the ART consistently votes high confidence regardless of plan quality, the confidence vote is not providing useful signal; investigate whether the vote is honest.
Best Practices
Prepare thoroughly:
The quality of PI Planning is primarily determined by the quality of the preparation: the business vision, the programme backlog, and the architecture briefing.
Keep team breakouts focused:
The team breakout phase should produce draft PI Objectives and a populated programme board, not a fully detailed sprint plan.
ROAM AI-specific risks explicitly:
Model training timelines, evaluation uncertainties, and data pipeline risks should be ROAM-ed alongside standard programme risks.
Track programme board weekly:
The programme board is a living artefact; the RTE should review it in every ART sync to monitor dependency resolution status.
Run a planning retrospective:
At the end of each PI Planning event, collect feedback on what worked and what could be improved for the next event.
Performance and Scalability

PI Planning for large ARTs—eight or more teams—requires careful facilitation and venue design to enable effective simultaneous team planning without disruption. Common scaling mechanisms include running team breakouts in separate rooms with a coordinated dependency-sharing session at defined intervals, rather than running all teams in a single large room. For remote PI Planning, dedicated virtual break-out spaces for each team with a shared virtual programme board visible to all teams is the standard approach.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Jira Advanced Roadmaps:
PI Planning boards with team capacity, sprint breakdowns, and dependency links; integrates PI Objectives with team-level story tracking.
Miro or MURAL:
Virtual programme boards for remote or hybrid PI Planning events; supports sticky notes, dependency arrows, and team breakout spaces.
SAFe (Scaled Agile):
The originating framework for PI Planning; provides training, certification, and a large community of practice.
Program Board templates:
Physical or virtual board templates for the PI Planning event that structure the dependency visualisation exercise.
Comparison with Alternatives
Monthly programme sync:
Lower overhead than PI Planning but insufficient for surfacing cross-team dependencies before they become blockers; dependencies discovered monthly rather than quarterly.
Quarterly roadmap update without team planning:
Aligns stakeholders on direction but does not produce team-level commitments or surface inter-team dependencies.
Continuous rolling planning:
More adaptive than PI Planning but requires constant coordination effort and may not provide sufficient planning horizon for AI work with long lead times.
PI Planning (this approach):
Best for programmes with significant cross-team dependencies and hard release boundaries; requires significant event investment but produces the strongest commitment and dependency alignment.
Security and Reliability

PI Planning events and their outputs—programme boards, PI Objectives, dependency lists—contain sensitive programme information about upcoming product capabilities, competitive initiatives, and resource allocation. Virtual PI Planning sessions should use access-controlled collaboration platforms with meeting recordings stored in secure systems. Programme boards and PI Objectives documents should be shared within the programme community rather than broadly accessible.
Key Takeaways
PI Planning is a time-boxed event where all ART teams plan the next twelve weeks together, commit to PI Objectives, and surface cross-team dependencies before the increment begins.
The quality of PI Planning is primarily determined by the quality of preparation: business vision, programme backlog, and architecture briefing.
For agentic AI portfolios, research spikes and model experiments need distinct planning approaches—time-box-based rather than story-point-based—to produce realistic commitments.
The programme board is the central coordination artefact of PI Planning; it must be maintained throughout the PI, not just created at the planning event.
Business owner presence throughout the event—not just for the opening presentation—enables real-time prioritisation decisions that improve plan quality.
A confidence vote below seventy percent is valuable data: it signals that teams do not believe the plan is achievable and requires additional planning to address.
Preparing the Programme Backlog for AI Work

The programme backlog that feeds PI Planning determines the quality of the plans that emerge. A backlog that is poorly prepared—with vague epics, missing acceptance criteria, and unresolved technical uncertainties—produces PI plans that cannot be reliably executed. For agentic AI portfolios, backlog preparation has additional requirements beyond conventional software development: research spikes must be defined with a clear hypothesis and decision output, model performance targets must be specified as measurable criteria, and data requirements must be documented and their availability confirmed.

Backlog refinement for AI programmes should happen in two streams: a product backlog refinement stream that focuses on user-facing features and their acceptance criteria, and a technical enabler refinement stream that focuses on platform capabilities, model improvements, and evaluation infrastructure. These streams can be refined separately by different stakeholders—product owners and business analysts for the product stream, system architects and AI engineers for the enabler stream—and then integrated into a single prioritised programme backlog before PI Planning.

A well-prepared programme backlog for an agentic AI programme includes three categories of work. Features are user-visible capabilities that can be demonstrated in a System Demo and have measurable acceptance criteria. Enablers are technical platform work—model improvements, infrastructure upgrades, evaluation harness enhancements—that do not directly deliver user-visible capabilities but are required to support future feature delivery. Research spikes are time-boxed experiments with a defined hypothesis and a decision as output. Each category has different acceptance criteria, different planning units, and different success metrics that must be accounted for in PI Planning.

# programme-backlog-template.yaml — backlog structure for agentic AI PI Planning
features:
  - id: "FEAT-2025-Q2-001"
    title: "Deploy intent routing agent to email channel"
    description: "Extend existing chat intent routing agent to handle incoming email queries"
    business_value: 9
    size_estimate: "L"  # XS | S | M | L | XL
    acceptance_criteria:
      - "Intent classification accuracy >= 88% on email test set"
      - "End-to-end response time <= 30 seconds for 95% of emails"
      - "Escalation to human agent for unrecognised intents working correctly"
    dependencies:
      - "Enabler: Email connector API ready (PLT-ENB-003)"
    team: "customer_automation"

enablers:
  - id: "PLT-ENB-003"
    title: "Email channel connector API"
    enabler_type: "infrastructure"
    description: "REST API adapter connecting email system to agent platform"
    technical_value: 8
    size_estimate: "M"
    acceptance_criteria:
      - "API handles 50 messages/minute sustained throughput"
      - "Authentication using OAuth 2.0"
      - "Full request/response logging for audit"
    team: "platform"

  - id: "PLT-ENB-004"
    title: "Model evaluation harness v2.0"
    enabler_type: "infrastructure"
    description: "Extended evaluation harness supporting multi-turn conversation evaluation"
    technical_value: 9
    size_estimate: "L"
    team: "platform"

research_spikes:
  - id: "SPIKE-2025-Q2-002"
    title: "Evaluate retrieval-augmented generation for product FAQ agent"
    hypothesis: "RAG approach will improve answer accuracy on long-tail FAQ questions by > 15% vs current fine-tuning approach"
    time_box_days: 10
    decision_output: "Written recommendation: proceed with RAG | stay with fine-tuning | hybrid approach"
    success_criteria: "Decision document with supporting evaluation data delivered by sprint 3"
    team: "knowledge_automation"

Backlog review sessions before PI Planning should explicitly validate three things for each planned backlog item: that the acceptance criteria are specific enough to be verifiable, that the technical approach is sufficiently understood that the team can commit to the sprint allocation, and that all prerequisites—data access, model infrastructure, regulatory approvals—are either confirmed or have explicit plans for how they will be secured before the work begins. Items that fail this validation should be deferred or refined before being brought into the PI plan.
Separate feature, enabler, and spike categories:
Each work type needs different planning, estimation, and acceptance criteria approaches.
Validate prerequisites before PI Planning:
Data access, infrastructure readiness, and regulatory clearances must be confirmed or have explicit plans before items are committed to the PI.
Include measurable AI performance criteria:
Features that involve AI components must have specific, measurable accuracy or performance acceptance criteria, not just functional descriptions.
Prepare one hundred fifty percent of capacity:
The programme backlog should contain work equivalent to one hundred fifty percent of the ART's expected PI capacity so teams have flexibility to adjust scope during breakouts.
Managing Distributed and Hybrid PI Planning

Many agentic AI programmes have geographically distributed teams that make in-person PI Planning impractical. Distributed PI Planning—conducted fully remotely or in a hybrid format with some participants in person and others remote—requires deliberate design to preserve the coordination benefits of the event while accommodating the limitations of remote participation.

The most important adaptation for distributed PI Planning is the programme board. In an in-person event, the programme board is a physical wall with sticky notes representing work items and strings or yarn connecting dependent items across team swim lanes. This physical artefact enables all teams to see each other's work and dependencies simultaneously, enabling rapid identification of conflicts and overlaps. For a distributed event, the programme board must be replicated in a digital collaboration tool—Miro, MURAL, or a purpose-built PI Planning tool—that enables all participants to see and interact with the board in real time.

Time zone management is a significant challenge for distributed PI Planning. A two-day event that works well for a single time zone may not work at all for teams spanning twelve hours of time difference. Practical adaptations include: running team breakouts asynchronously over a forty-eight-hour window, scheduling the synchronous elements—business vision, architecture briefing, dependency resolution, confidence vote—in overlapping hours that all teams can attend, and rotating the inconvenient schedule across PIs so no single team consistently bears the burden of the most difficult time slot.
Digital programme board:
Use Miro, MURAL, or equivalent for remote teams; configure it before the event and test access for all participants.
Time zone management:
Schedule synchronous elements in overlapping hours; run breakouts asynchronously if time zones are extreme.
Camera-on norm:
Remote PI Planning events where participants keep cameras on have significantly higher engagement and collaboration quality.
Breakout room coordination:
Pre-configure virtual breakout rooms for each team and for cross-team dependency resolution sessions.
Record the business and architecture sessions:
Make recordings available for participants who cannot attend the synchronous elements in their time zone.
